questions.question_text:
In a 2022 study, Nicolás M. Morato and colleagues attempted to resolve a long-standing paradox: although water is
critical to most reactions that enable life, during the earliest stages of Earth's history —when most of the planet's
surface was covered by oceans — the presence of water likely -
the formation of proteins and other
biomolecules that are found in all living organisms.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase

choices.A: reflected
choices.B: hampered
choices.C: imitated
choices.D: exploited

selected_incorrect_answer.text: imitated

correct_answer.text: hampered

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: word-in-context and precision vocabulary

answer_explanation.why_correct: The sentence sets up a paradox: water was necessary for life, but when Earth was covered by oceans it likely hindered the formation of early biomolecules. "Hampered" is the only choice that matches that negative cause-and-effect idea.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: "Imitated" means copied or mimicked, which does not make sense with "the formation of proteins and other biomolecules." The sentence needs a verb meaning interfered with, not resembled.
answer_explanation.takeaway: Use contrast clues like "although" and the stated paradox to predict whether the blank should be positive or negative before reading the options.

questions.question_text:
In a study of desert cyanobacteria, researchers confronted a familiar paradox: although intense sunlight supplies the energy these organisms need for photosynthesis, during prolonged droughts the accompanying ultraviolet exposure likely ___ the repair of DNA damage in colonies growing on exposed rock.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?

choices.A: chronicled
choices.B: hindered
choices.C: mirrored
choices.D: rewarded

selected_incorrect_answer.text: mirrored

correct_answer.text: hindered

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: word-in-context and precision vocabulary

answer_explanation.why_correct: The sentence presents a paradox: sunlight is useful, but the ultraviolet exposure that comes with it interferes with DNA repair. "Hindered" fits that negative cause-and-effect relationship.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: "Mirrored" means reflected or resembled. The sentence needs a verb meaning impeded the repair process, not one suggesting similarity.
answer_explanation.takeaway: In paradox questions, the blank usually captures the surprising downside named after the contrast word.

questions.question_text:
Conservators working with fragile manuscripts face another paradox: although circulating air can help prevent mold, in archives with extremely low humidity that same airflow likely ___ the preservation of brittle paper and faded ink.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?

choices.A: cataloged
choices.B: undermined
choices.C: echoed
choices.D: praised

selected_incorrect_answer.text: echoed

correct_answer.text: undermined

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: word-in-context and precision vocabulary

answer_explanation.why_correct: The passage says airflow helps in one way but harms preservation in very dry conditions. "Undermined" precisely conveys that it worked against preservation.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: "Echoed" has no logical connection to preserving paper and ink. The sentence needs a verb showing damage or interference.
answer_explanation.takeaway: When a sentence says something is helpful in one respect but harmful in another, predict a negative verb before checking the choices.

questions.question_text:
A casual description of Scherezade García's 2019 mural Blame It on the Bean: The Power of Coffee can make the
work seem
_— a painting that is housed in a coffee shop and that depicts three women drinking coffee may not
sound particularly ambitious - but in fact the work is a complex, dynamic meditation on gender and the legacy of
colonialism that demands serious attention.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?

choices.A: unassuming
choices.B: shrewd
choices.C: incongruous
choices.D: pretentious

selected_incorrect_answer.text: incongruous

correct_answer.text: unassuming

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: word-in-context and precision vocabulary

answer_explanation.why_correct: The next clause says the mural may not sound ambitious at first, even though it is actually complex and demanding. "Unassuming" means modest or not immediately impressive, which fits that contrast.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: "Incongruous" means out of place or not in harmony. The issue is not that the mural sounds mismatched; it is that a casual description makes it seem simpler and less ambitious than it really is.
answer_explanation.takeaway: When a sentence pivots with "but in fact," choose the word that best describes the first, mistaken impression.

questions.question_text:
A brief description of Maya Ito's installation Tide Ledger can make the work seem ___ - a room lined with shipping receipts and weathered ledgers may not sound especially ambitious - but in fact the installation is a layered reflection on migration, trade, and environmental loss.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?

choices.A: unassuming
choices.B: calculating
choices.C: incongruous
choices.D: pretentious

selected_incorrect_answer.text: incongruous

correct_answer.text: unassuming

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: word-in-context and precision vocabulary

answer_explanation.why_correct: The sentence contrasts an initially modest impression with the revelation that the work is actually ambitious and complex. "Unassuming" captures that first impression.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: "Incongruous" means out of place or mismatched. The description does not make the installation seem mismatched; it makes it seem simpler than it really is.
answer_explanation.takeaway: If the sentence pivots from a plain first impression to a richer reality, the blank should describe the plain impression, not a mismatch.

questions.question_text:
At first glance, Joaquin Mendez's short film Quiet Block can seem ___ - a sequence of static shots of apartment windows may not sound particularly daring - but the film turns out to be an intricate study of surveillance, privacy, and urban loneliness.
Which choice completes the text with the most logical and precise word or phrase?

choices.A: modest
choices.B: tactical
choices.C: incongruous
choices.D: self-important

selected_incorrect_answer.text: incongruous

correct_answer.text: modest

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: word-in-context and precision vocabulary

answer_explanation.why_correct: The setup says the film may not sound ambitious at first, so the blank should mean not immediately impressive. "Modest" fits that idea.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: "Incongruous" would suggest the film sounds oddly out of place, but the passage is about understatement, not mismatch.
answer_explanation.takeaway: Use the clause after the dash to define the mistaken first impression as precisely as possible.

questions.question_text:
Following the eradication of the gray wolf in Yellowstone National Park in 1926, the population of elk — a primary
prey of the gray wolf -exceeded a healthy size for the park's ecosystem. Elk overpopulation led to overgrazing of
areas that a multitude of other animals relied on for food and shelter. As scientists began to see how essential the
gray wolf was to the Yellowstone food chain, ecological restoration strategies were employed to reintroduce the gray
wolf to the park in 1996. The rebound effect in the park's natural ecosystem was noticed almost immediately.
Which choice best describes the overall structure of the text?

choices.A: It summarizes a problem that developed in Yellowstone National Park in the 1920s and then offers potential solutions to that problem.
choices.B: It mentions the elimination of the gray wolf from Yellowstone National Park and then explains why the wolf was eventually restored to the park.
choices.C: It presents a claim about the health of the Yellowstone National Park gray wolf population and then gives specific examples to support that claim.
choices.D: It explains why Yellowstone National Park allowed the eradication of the gray wolf and then discusses the consequences of reintroducing the wolf to the park.

selected_incorrect_answer.text: It explains why Yellowstone National Park allowed the eradication of the gray wolf and then discusses the consequences of reintroducing the wolf to the park.

correct_answer.text: It mentions the elimination of the gray wolf from Yellowstone National Park and then explains why the wolf was eventually restored to the park.

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: passage structure, sentence function, and author purpose

answer_explanation.why_correct: The passage first tells what happened when wolves were eradicated, then explains that scientists recognized the wolves' ecological importance and reintroduced them. Choice B captures that two-part structure without adding motives the text never gives.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice D invents an explanation for why Yellowstone allowed eradication, but the passage never gives one. It only states that the wolves were eradicated and later restored because they were essential to the food chain.
answer_explanation.takeaway: For structure questions, summarize each sentence's job in order and reject choices that add causes, motives, or judgments the passage does not state.

questions.question_text:
After a coastal city drained much of its marshland in the 1930s, seasonal flooding became more severe and bird populations declined. As environmental scientists documented the marshes' role in absorbing stormwater and supporting wildlife, local officials began restoring wetland areas. Within a few years, flood damage had lessened noticeably.
Which choice best describes the overall structure of the text?

choices.A: It summarizes a problem that developed after the city drained its marshland and then offers several possible solutions to that problem.
choices.B: It mentions the draining of the marshland and then explains why the marshes were eventually restored.
choices.C: It presents a claim about the health of the marshland and then gives specific examples to support that claim.
choices.D: It explains why city leaders chose to drain the marshland and then discusses the consequences of restoring it.

selected_incorrect_answer.text: It explains why city leaders chose to drain the marshland and then discusses the consequences of restoring it.

correct_answer.text: It mentions the draining of the marshland and then explains why the marshes were eventually restored.

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: passage structure, sentence function, and author purpose

answer_explanation.why_correct: The passage first states that the marshes were drained, then explains that scientists recognized their value and officials restored them. Choice B matches that sequence exactly.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice D adds a reason city leaders drained the marshland, but the passage never gives one. It only describes what happened and why restoration followed.
answer_explanation.takeaway: For structure questions, map the passage in order and avoid answers that invent a motive for an earlier event.

questions.question_text:
When a school district sharply reduced recess time in the 1990s, teachers soon reported more classroom restlessness and fewer opportunities for informal peer interaction. After child-development researchers emphasized the value of unstructured play, the district reinstated longer recess periods. Reports of student conflict and inattention then declined.
Which choice best describes the overall structure of the text?

choices.A: It summarizes a problem that arose after recess was reduced and then proposes a range of possible fixes for that problem.
choices.B: It mentions the reduction of recess and then explains why longer recess was eventually reinstated.
choices.C: It presents a claim about the benefits of recess and then offers several anecdotes to support that claim.
choices.D: It explains why administrators decided to reduce recess and then discusses the consequences of bringing it back.

selected_incorrect_answer.text: It explains why administrators decided to reduce recess and then discusses the consequences of bringing it back.

correct_answer.text: It mentions the reduction of recess and then explains why longer recess was eventually reinstated.

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: passage structure, sentence function, and author purpose

answer_explanation.why_correct: The text first describes recess being reduced, then explains that research about play led the district to restore it. Choice B captures the passage structure cleanly.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice D wrongly says the passage explains why administrators reduced recess. That cause is never supplied.
answer_explanation.takeaway: If the passage gives only the reason for a later reversal, do not pick an answer that claims it also explains the original decision.

questions.question_text:
Curious about how people visually perceive objects in their dreams, Stephen LaBerge and team recruited lucid
dreamers— people aware that they're dreaming as it's happening- for a research study. These participants were
reliably able to signal when they had entered a dream state; the team then observed participants' eye movements as
they slept. The smoothness with which participants' eyes tracked objects in their dreams closely matched how
sighted people who are awake visually track objects around them, suggesting to the team that the brain perceives
dream objects as the product of something other than pure imagination.
Which choice best states the function of the underlined portion in the text as a whole?

choices.A: To offer key evidence that undermines LaBerge and team's initial hypothesis
choices.B: To show the unexpected result that led LaBerge and team to change the focus of their study
choices.C: To illustrate an important real-world implication of LaBerge and team's main finding
choices.D: To identify a comparable circumstance that helps justify LaBerge and team's conclusion

selected_incorrect_answer.text: To offer key evidence that undermines LaBerge and team's initial hypothesis

correct_answer.text: To identify a comparable circumstance that helps justify LaBerge and team's conclusion

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: passage structure, sentence function, and author purpose

answer_explanation.why_correct: The underlined comparison to how awake, sighted people track real objects gives LaBerge's team a concrete point of reference. That comparable real-world behavior helps support the conclusion that dream objects are being perceived rather than merely imagined.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice A reverses the logic of the paragraph. The comparison is evidence for the team's conclusion, not evidence that undermines an initial hypothesis.
answer_explanation.takeaway: On function questions, ask what the underlined part is doing right there: evidence, comparison, qualification, or implication.

questions.question_text:
Curious about how people mentally navigate spaces they have already explored, Elena Park and team asked volunteers to walk through a virtual maze and later imagine retracing the route while following moving dots on a screen. Participants' hand motions during the imagery task were highly consistent. [Underlined: The steadiness of those motions closely matched how experienced hikers track bends on a marked trail in daylight,] suggesting to the team that imagined navigation recruits systems used in real spatial perception.
Which choice best states the function of the underlined portion in the text as a whole?

choices.A: To offer key evidence that undermines Park and team's initial hypothesis
choices.B: To show the unexpected result that led Park and team to change the focus of their study
choices.C: To illustrate an important real-world implication of Park and team's main finding
choices.D: To identify a comparable circumstance that helps justify Park and team's conclusion

selected_incorrect_answer.text: To offer key evidence that undermines Park and team's initial hypothesis

correct_answer.text: To identify a comparable circumstance that helps justify Park and team's conclusion

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: passage structure, sentence function, and author purpose

answer_explanation.why_correct: The underlined comparison supplies a real-world analog: imagined navigation resembles real navigation by hikers. That comparable circumstance helps support the researchers' conclusion.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice A gets the direction wrong. The comparison strengthens the conclusion instead of undermining any earlier hypothesis.
answer_explanation.takeaway: When a sentence says one behavior matched another familiar behavior, it is usually providing supporting comparison evidence.

questions.question_text:
To learn how musicians hear notes internally, Priya Raman and colleagues asked trained pianists to study short melodies and then silently imagine replaying them while tapping a key in time. The timing of the taps proved unusually regular. [Underlined: That timing closely matched how pianists keep tempo when actually performing a memorized passage,] suggesting that imagined listening relies on mechanisms related to real musical perception.
Which choice best states the function of the underlined portion in the text as a whole?

choices.A: To offer key evidence that undermines Raman and colleagues' initial hypothesis
choices.B: To show the unexpected result that led Raman and colleagues to change the focus of their study
choices.C: To illustrate an important real-world implication of Raman and colleagues' main finding
choices.D: To identify a comparable circumstance that helps justify Raman and colleagues' conclusion

selected_incorrect_answer.text: To offer key evidence that undermines Raman and colleagues' initial hypothesis

correct_answer.text: To identify a comparable circumstance that helps justify Raman and colleagues' conclusion

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: passage structure, sentence function, and author purpose

answer_explanation.why_correct: The underlined part compares silent imagined timing to actual performance timing. That similarity is evidence supporting the conclusion about real perception systems.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Nothing in the paragraph suggests the comparison undermines the study. It is presented as confirming evidence.
answer_explanation.takeaway: On function questions, distinguish between evidence that supports a conclusion and evidence that contradicts it.

questions.question_text:
Text 1
Good art often challenges and disrupts social and aesthetic norms, but the creation of public art — paintings,
sculptures, and performance pieces displayed in nonmuseum or nontheatrical public settings - typically requires
broad agreement among artists, civic officials, and community members about the works' message and artistic goals.
Public art that fails to appease everyone by being sufficiently aesthetically and conceptually bland almost inevitably.
provokes backlash.
Text 2
Public art is commonly displayed in spaces intended for purposes other than meaningful aesthetic engagement. Some
critics of public art therefore note that norm-defying pieces that aren't effectively integrated within their
surroundings in a manner that primes passersby to appreciate the pieces' merits (as is often the case) tend to be
regarded more unfavorably than similarly provocative art encountered in museums is.
Based on the texts, how would the critics mentioned in Text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in Text 1?

choices.A: By arguing that the reason members of the general public might disagree about a public artwork's merits is unrelated to the unconventionality of its appearance and ideas
choices.B: By agreeing with the idea that only works of art that are universally appealing are suitable for displaying in public spaces
choices.C: By disputing the notion that civic leaders and community members are easily placated by art that is intended mainly to reinforce social norms
choices.D: By contending that the kinds of reactions controversial public artworks often receive aren't exclusively the result of attributes inherent in the works themselves

selected_incorrect_answer.text: By disputing the notion that civic leaders and community members are easily placated by art that is intended mainly to reinforce social norms

correct_answer.text: By contending that the kinds of reactions controversial public artworks often receive aren't exclusively the result of attributes inherent in the works themselves

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: paired-text response and disagreement

answer_explanation.why_correct: Text 1 suggests backlash comes from the artwork's norm-defying nature, but Text 2 says negative reactions also depend on whether the piece is effectively integrated into its surroundings. That means the critics in Text 2 would argue that backlash is not caused only by the work's inherent qualities.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice C shifts the issue to whether bland, norm-reinforcing art placates people. Text 2 does not discuss that claim; it focuses on context and presentation as additional causes of backlash.
answer_explanation.takeaway: In paired-text questions, isolate the exact point of disagreement in one sentence before testing the answer choices.

questions.question_text:
Text 1
Experimental murals in train stations often spark public complaints because commuters expect agreeable decoration rather than challenging imagery. In this view, murals that are visually and conceptually disruptive almost inevitably provoke backlash.
Text 2
Some critics argue that station murals are judged harshly partly because they appear in settings built for speed and transit rather than reflection. They note that similarly challenging works displayed in galleries are often received more favorably when viewers are given cues that invite close attention.
Based on the texts, how would the critics mentioned in Text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in Text 1?

choices.A: By arguing that the reason members of the general public might disagree about a public artwork's merits is unrelated to the unconventionality of its appearance and ideas
choices.B: By agreeing with the idea that only works of art that are universally appealing are suitable for displaying in public spaces
choices.C: By disputing the notion that civic leaders and community members are easily placated by art that is intended mainly to reinforce social norms
choices.D: By contending that the kinds of reactions controversial public artworks often receive aren't exclusively the result of attributes inherent in the works themselves

selected_incorrect_answer.text: By disputing the notion that civic leaders and community members are easily placated by art that is intended mainly to reinforce social norms

correct_answer.text: By contending that the kinds of reactions controversial public artworks often receive aren't exclusively the result of attributes inherent in the works themselves

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: paired-text response and disagreement

answer_explanation.why_correct: Text 2 says reaction depends not just on the work itself but also on the context in which people encounter it. That means backlash is not solely the result of the mural's disruptive qualities.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice C switches the issue to whether bland art placates people. Text 2 never takes up that point; it focuses on context and audience preparation.
answer_explanation.takeaway: Find the precise place where Text 2 qualifies Text 1. Here, the qualification is about context, not about bland art.

questions.question_text:
Text 1
Avant-garde theater staged in city plazas often draws criticism because the performances reject the familiar pacing and storytelling that public audiences expect. According to this view, plaza performances that refuse to be accessible almost inevitably invite hostility.
Text 2
Other critics respond that public theater is frequently encountered by people who were not seeking out a performance at all. They argue that unconventional productions receive especially negative reactions when the surrounding space offers no cues about how to watch or interpret them, unlike theatergoers who enter a playhouse prepared to engage.
Based on the texts, how would the critics mentioned in Text 2 most likely respond to the underlined claim in Text 1?

choices.A: By arguing that the reason members of the general public might disagree about a public artwork's merits is unrelated to the unconventionality of its appearance and ideas
choices.B: By agreeing with the idea that only works of art that are universally appealing are suitable for displaying in public spaces
choices.C: By disputing the notion that civic leaders and community members are easily placated by art that is intended mainly to reinforce social norms
choices.D: By contending that the kinds of reactions controversial public artworks often receive aren't exclusively the result of attributes inherent in the works themselves

selected_incorrect_answer.text: By disputing the notion that civic leaders and community members are easily placated by art that is intended mainly to reinforce social norms

correct_answer.text: By contending that the kinds of reactions controversial public artworks often receive aren't exclusively the result of attributes inherent in the works themselves

questions.study_category_label: Craft and Structure: paired-text response and disagreement

answer_explanation.why_correct: Text 2 argues that setting and audience expectation also shape reactions, so hostility is not caused only by the production's unconventional features.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice C is about whether safe, conventional work satisfies people. Text 2 instead emphasizes viewing conditions and context.
answer_explanation.takeaway: In paired-text disagreement questions, distinguish the topic from the exact causal claim being challenged.

Domestic sheep's wild ancestor, the mouflon, has a coarse outer coat and an inner coat of wool fiber that is finer in
diameter and therefore much softer. In some domestic breeds, such as the Spanish Merino, the outer fiber is only
marginally coarser than the inner, and the wool is soft overall. Thus, Merino wool is ideal for delicate garments worn
against the skin. Meanwhile, the Navajo-Churro has been selected to retain the marked distinction between outer and
inner fiber that the Merino has lost. Being coarser than Merino wool overall, Navajo-Churro wool yields a more
durable yarn, which Diné (Navajo) weavers use in their celebrated rugs. Yet a comparison of the fiber characteristics
of all three sheep reveals that
Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to complete the text?

choices.A: the selection process that enabled the Navajo-Churro to retain its somewhat coarse outer fiber also resulted in inner fiber that, at its softest, is softer than either the mouflon's or the Merino's inner fiber.
choices.B: the Navajo-Churro more closely resembles its ancestor, the mouflon, in the uniform softness of its inner fiber, while the Merino more closely resembles the mouflon in the variable diameter of its outer fiber.
choices.C: domestication resulted in a counterintuitive increase in the inner fiber's minimum diameter, making the inner fiber of the Merino and the Navajo-Churro less suitable for delicate garments than the mouflon's inner fiber is.
choices.D: the domestication of the mouflon and the subsequent selection process that produced the Merino and the Navajo-Churro resulted in greater softness of outer and inner fiber alike.

selected_incorrect_answer.text: domestication resulted in a counterintuitive increase in the inner fiber's minimum diameter, making the inner fiber of the Merino and the Navajo-Churro less suitable for delicate garments than the mouflon's inner fiber is.

correct_answer.text: the selection process that enabled the Navajo-Churro to retain its somewhat coarse outer fiber also resulted in inner fiber that, at its softest, is softer than either the mouflon's or the Merino's inner fiber.

questions.study_category_label: Information and Ideas: tables, charts, and quantitative comparison

answer_explanation.why_correct: The table shows Navajo-Churro inner fibers range from 10 to 35 microns, so at their softest they are 10 microns wide. That is finer than mouflon inner fibers at 15 microns and finer than Merino inner fibers at 17 to 21 microns, so choice A is fully supported.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice C says domestication increased the minimum inner-fiber diameter in both domestic breeds, but Navajo-Churro's minimum is 10, which is lower than the mouflon's 15. That directly contradicts the table.
answer_explanation.takeaway: For table questions, check every comparative word in an answer against the numbers one by one, especially minimums, maximums, and exceptions.

The wild European rabbit has a coarse outer coat and a softer undercoat. In some domesticated breeds, such as the Valley Angora, the outer fiber is only slightly coarser than the undercoat, making the fur soft overall. Meanwhile, the Highland Angora has been bred to retain a sharper distinction between outer and inner fibers. Yet a comparison of all three rabbits reveals that
Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to complete the text?

choices.A: the breeding process that allowed the Highland Angora to retain its coarse guard hairs also resulted in undercoat fibers that, at their finest, are finer than either the wild rabbit's or the Valley Angora's undercoat fibers.
choices.B: the Highland Angora more closely resembles its wild ancestor in the uniform softness of its undercoat fibers, while the Valley Angora more closely resembles the wild rabbit in the variability of its guard hairs.
choices.C: domestication resulted in a counterintuitive increase in the undercoat's minimum diameter, making the undercoat of both domestic breeds less soft than the wild rabbit's undercoat.

selected_incorrect_answer.text: domestication resulted in a counterintuitive increase in the undercoat's minimum diameter, making the undercoat of both domestic breeds less soft than the wild rabbit's undercoat.

correct_answer.text: the breeding process that allowed the Highland Angora to retain its coarse guard hairs also resulted in undercoat fibers that, at their finest, are finer than either the wild rabbit's or the Valley Angora's undercoat fibers.

questions.study_category_label: Information and Ideas: tables, charts, and quantitative comparison

answer_explanation.why_correct: Highland Angora undercoat fibers range down to 11 microns, which is finer than the wild rabbit's 18-micron undercoat and the Valley Angora's 16-to-19-micron range. Choice A states that relationship accurately.
answer_explanation.why_selected_answer_is_wrong: Choice C claims domestication increased the minimum undercoat diameter in both domestic breeds, but Highland Angora has a lower minimum than the wild rabbit. The table directly disproves that claim.
answer_explanation.takeaway: Range questions turn on the endpoints. Always compare the minimum and maximum values explicitly before choosing.

The Heritage Cashmere, however, has been bred to preserve a strong contrast between outer and inner fibers. Yet a comparison of all three goats reveals that

questions.study_category_label: Information and Ideas: tables, charts, and quantitative comparison

answer_explanation.why_correct: Heritage Cashmere inner fibers go as low as 9 microns, below the wild ibex's 14 and the Silver Cashmere's 15-to-19 range. Choice A is the only answer that reflects that comparison correctly.
